---
title: "Organize Data Notebook"
---
#| label: load-data
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(modelsummary)
library(gt)
library(here)
library(fixest)

```{r}
#label: timestamp-start

timestamp()
```

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(here)
source(here("utils","check_packages.R"))
source(here("utils","functions.R"))
```

This quarto doc is used to organize the data.

# Load the Data

```{r}

rps <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw", "RPS.xlsx"))
generation <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw", "generation.xls"))

```

```{r}
# clean rps
rps <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw", "RPS.xlsx"), skip = 24)
rps <- rps |> 
  rename(state = State, rps_tier = `RPS Tier or Carve Out`) |>
  #  Total RPS
  filter(str_detect(rps_tier, "^Total RPS")) |>
  select(state, starts_with("20")) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("20"), names_to = "year", 
               values_to = "rps_total") |>
  mutate(year = as.numeric(year))

# clean 2: reverting states names from abbreviation to full name 
rps <- rps |>
  mutate(
    state = recode(state,
      !!!setNames(state.name, state.abb),
      "DC" = "District of Columbia"
    )
  )

# clean rps 3: Keep states that have rps data
states_keep <- c(
  "Arizona", "California", "Colorado", "Connecticut",
  "Delaware", "Hawaii", "Iowa", "Illinois", "Kansas",
  "Massachusetts", "Maryland", "Maine", "Michigan",
  "Minnesota", "Missouri", "Montana", "North Carolina",
  "Nebraska", "New Hampshire", "New Jersey", "New Mexico",
  "Nevada", "New York", "Ohio", "Oregon", "Pennsylvania",
  "Rhode Island", "Texas", "Virginia", "Washington",
  "Wisconsin", "District of Columbia"
)

rps <- rps |>
  filter(
    state %in% states_keep,
    year %in% c(2008, 2009)
  )
```


```{r}
# clean renewable generation  
generation <- read_excel(here("data", "data_raw", "generation.xls"), skip = 6)
 

# clean 2
generation <- generation |>
  select(State, `2008`, `...3`, `2009`, `...8`) |>
  rename(
    state = State,
    total_generation_2008 = `2008`,
    percent_renewable_2008 = `...3`,
    total_generation_2009 = `2009`,
    percent_renewable_2009 = `...8`
  )

# clean 3
generation_long <- generation |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -state,
    names_to = c(".value", "year"),
    names_pattern = "(.*)_(\\d{4})"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = as.numeric(year),
    total_generation = as.numeric(total_generation),
    percent_renewable = as.numeric(percent_renewable)
  )

# clean 4: keep states that have RPS data avaialble
states_keep <- c(
  "Arizona",
  "California",
  "Colorado",
  "Connecticut",
  "DC",
  "Delaware",
  "Hawaii",
  "Iowa",
  "Illinois",
  "Kansas",
  "Massachusetts",
  "Maryland",
  "Maine",
  "Michigan",
  "Minnesota",
  "Missouri",
  "Montana",
  "North Carolina",
  "Nebraska",
  "New Hampshire",
  "New Jersey",
  "New Mexico",
  "Nevada",
  "New York",
  "Ohio",
  "Oregon",
  "Pennsylvania",
  "Rhode Island",
  "Texas",
  "Virginia",
  "Washington",
  "Wisconsin"
)

generation <- generation |>
  filter(state %in% states_keep)

sort(unique(generation$state))
```

```{r}
# merge rps and generation and craete final dateset 
energy_data <- rps |>
  left_join(
    generation_long,
    by = c("state", "year")
  )
```

```{r}
# handling missing values 
energy_data |>
  summarise(
    across(
      everything(),
      ~ sum(is.na(.))
    )
  )

# missing values 
energy_data |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "n_missing"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(n_missing))

#
energy_data |>
  filter(
    is.na(total_generation) |
    is.na(percent_renewable)
  )

# missing values 
energy_data <- energy_data |>
  drop_na(total_generation, percent_renewable)
```


```{r}
# chnage to percentage 
energy_data <- energy_data |>
  mutate(
    rps_percent = rps_total * 100
  )
# save data
save(
  energy_data,
  file = here(
    "data",
    "data_constructed",
    "analytical_data.RData"
  )
)
```


```{r}
# analysis 

load(
  here(
    "data",
    "data_constructed",
    "analytical_data.RData"
  )
)
```

```{r}
# Descriptive Statistics
library(dplyr)

energy_data |>
  summarise(
    mean_renewable = mean(percent_renewable),
    sd_renewable = sd(percent_renewable),
    mean_rps = mean(rps_percent),
    sd_rps = sd(rps_percent)
  )
```

```{r}
# Descriptive statistics 
datasummary_skim(
  energy_data
)

# The analytical dataset contains 60 state-year observations representing 30 U.S. states observed in 2008 and 2009. The dataset combines information on state Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS) with electricity generation data to examine the relationship between renewable energy policy and renewable electricity production. RPS requirements vary considerably across states, ranging from 0% to approximately 35% of total electricity generation. The average RPS requirement is 5.3% (SD = 8.4), indicating substantial differences in the strength of renewable energy mandates among states during the study period.

Renewable electricity generation also exhibits substantial variation across states. The share of electricity generated from renewable sources ranges from less than 1% to nearly 75% of total generation, with a mean of 13.7% (SD = 18.6). The median renewable generation share is only 5.8%, substantially lower than the mean, suggesting a positively skewed distribution in which a relatively small number of states account for disproportionately high levels of renewable electricity production. This wide variation in both renewable generation and RPS requirements provides a useful foundation for investigating whether stronger renewable energy mandates are associated with higher levels of renewable electricity generation.

```
```{r}
ggplot(
  energy_data,
  aes(
    x = rps_total,
    y = percent_renewable
  )
) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    se = TRUE
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Renewable Portfolio Standard",
    y = "Renewable Generation Share",
    title = "Relationship between RPS and Renewable Generation"
  )

Figure 1 presents a scatterplot of state Renewable Portfolio Standards and renewable electricity generation shares. The fitted regression line suggests a positive relationship between the two variables, indicating that states with stronger RPS requirements tend to generate a larger share of their electricity from renewable sources. However, the relationship is not particularly strong. Several states with very low or zero RPS requirements nevertheless exhibit high renewable generation shares, likely reflecting favorable natural resource endowments such as hydropower availability. This pattern suggests that factors beyond policy mandates also influence renewable electricity generation outcomes.
```


```{r}
# Figure 2
ggplot(
  energy_data,
  aes(
    factor(year),
    percent_renewable
  )
) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Renewable Generation Share"
  )
```

```{r}
# Basic OLS model; Bivariate Relationship
model1 <- lm(
  percent_renewable ~ rps_percent,
  data = energy_data
)
summary(model1)


# Interpreting result of OLS Model
To examine the relationship between Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS) and renewable electricity generation, an ordinary least squares (OLS) regression model was estimated with renewable electricity generation share as the dependent variable and RPS requirements as the independent variable. The results indicate a positive and statistically significant association between the two variables. The estimated coefficient for RPS is 58.65 (p = .041), suggesting that a one-unit increase in the RPS proportion is associated with an increase of approximately 58.7 percentage points in renewable electricity generation. Because RPS is measured as a proportion ranging from 0 to 1, a more substantively meaningful interpretation is that a 10 percentage-point increase in a state's RPS requirement is associated with approximately a 5.9 percentage-point increase in the share of electricity generated from renewable sources. The overall model is statistically significant (F(1,58) = 4.38, p = .041), providing evidence of a positive relationship between renewable energy mandates and renewable electricity production.
Although the relationship is statistically significant, the model explains only a modest portion of the variation in renewable electricity generation across states (R² = .070; Adjusted R² = .054). This indicates that RPS requirements account for approximately 7 percent of the observed variation in renewable electricity generation, while most of the variation is explained by factors not included in the model. Differences in natural resource endowments, geographic conditions, energy market structures, historical energy infrastructure, and state economic characteristics may all influence renewable electricity production. Consequently, the findings suggest that stronger RPS policies are associated with higher levels of renewable generation, but that renewable energy outcomes are also shaped by a broader set of economic, political, and environmental factors.
```


```{r}
# State Fixed Effect Model 
model2 <- feols(
  percent_renewable ~ rps_percent | state,
  data = energy_data
)

summary(model2)

# Interpretation
A state fixed-effects model was estimated to examine whether changes in Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS) within states were associated with changes in renewable electricity generation between 2008 and 2009. By including state fixed effects, the model controls for time-invariant state characteristics, such as geography, natural resource endowments, historical energy infrastructure, and political culture, that could influence renewable energy generation.
The estimated coefficient for RPS requirements is positive (β = 0.266), indicating that a one-percentage-point increase in a state's RPS target is associated with an estimated 0.27 percentage-point increase in the share of electricity generated from renewable sources. However, this relationship is not statistically significant at conventional levels (p = .188). As a result, the analysis does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that changes in RPS requirements were associated with changes in renewable electricity generation within states during the study period. Although the direction of the coefficient remains consistent with theoretical expectations and the positive relationship observed in the OLS model, the magnitude of the estimated effect is smaller and no longer statistically distinguishable from zero once state-specific characteristics are taken into account.
The difference between the OLS and fixed-effects results suggests that part of the positive association observed in the OLS model may be driven by persistent differences across states rather than by policy changes within states. States with stronger renewable energy policies may also possess characteristics that make renewable energy development more likely, such as favorable renewable resources, supportive political environments, or prior investments in clean energy infrastructure. After accounting for these stable state characteristics, the estimated relationship between RPS requirements and renewable electricity generation becomes weaker and statistically insignificant.
```

```{r}
# State + Year fixed effect 
model3 <- feols(
  percent_renewable ~ rps_percent | state + year,
  data = energy_data
)

summary(model3)

# Interpretation 
A state and year fixed-effects model was estimated to further examine the relationship between Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS) and renewable electricity generation. This specification controls not only for time-invariant differences across states but also for factors that changed uniformly across all states between 2008 and 2009, such as national economic conditions, energy market trends, and broader developments in renewable energy technologies.

The results indicate that the coefficient for RPS requirements is negative but very small in magnitude (β = -0.045, p = .815). Specifically, a one-percentage-point increase in a state's RPS requirement is associated with an estimated 0.05 percentage-point decrease in the share of electricity generated from renewable sources. However, this relationship is not statistically significant and is substantively negligible. The large p-value indicates that the estimated effect is statistically indistinguishable from zero.

Compared to the OLS and state fixed-effects models, the coefficient becomes smaller and changes sign after accounting for both state-specific and year-specific factors. This suggests that the positive association observed in the baseline OLS model may largely reflect differences between states and broader temporal trends rather than the direct effect of short-term changes in RPS requirements. Consequently, the analysis does not provide evidence that increases in RPS targets between 2008 and 2009 led to measurable increases in renewable electricity generation over the same period.

Several factors may explain this finding. First, renewable energy investments often require substantial planning, financing, permitting, and construction, meaning that policy changes may take several years to influence electricity generation outcomes. Second, the dataset covers only two years, providing limited within-state variation in policy adoption and implementation. Finally, renewable energy generation is influenced by numerous factors beyond policy mandates, including resource availability, electricity demand, existing infrastructure, and market conditions. Therefore, while RPS policies may contribute to renewable energy development over longer time horizons, the present analysis does not detect a statistically significant short-term effect during the 2008–2009 period.

```

```{r}
# controling for total electricity generation (year + state + control)
model4 <- feols(
  percent_renewable ~ rps_percent + total_generation |
  state + year,
  data = energy_data
)

summary(model4)

# Interpretation
A final model was estimated that included both state and year fixed effects as well as a control for total electricity generation. The inclusion of total generation accounts for differences in the scale of electricity production across states and over time, allowing for a more stringent assessment of whether Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS) are associated with changes in renewable electricity generation.

The results indicate that neither RPS requirements nor total electricity generation are significantly associated with the share of electricity generated from renewable sources. The coefficient for RPS requirements is small and negative (β = -0.055, p = .783), suggesting that a one-percentage-point increase in a state's RPS target is associated with an estimated 0.06 percentage-point decrease in renewable electricity generation share. However, this estimate is not statistically significant and is substantively close to zero. Similarly, total electricity generation is not significantly related to renewable generation share (β = 3.26 × 10⁻⁸, p = .644), indicating that changes in the overall volume of electricity generation do not appear to explain variation in renewable electricity shares within states during the study period.

Compared to the previous models, the inclusion of the control variable does not materially alter the results. The coefficient for RPS requirements remains statistically insignificant and close to zero, suggesting that the absence of a detectable policy effect is not driven by differences in the scale of state electricity generation. Overall, the findings provide no evidence that short-term changes in RPS requirements between 2008 and 2009 were associated with measurable changes in renewable electricity generation after accounting for state-specific characteristics, year-specific effects, and variation in total electricity production.

```





```{r}
#label: timestamp-end

timestamp()
```
